title: Valid Properties of Anisotropic Mesh Generation
content: The anisotropic Delaunay triangulation is rigorously defined by a symmetric positive-definite metric tensor $M(x)$ via a length functional $L_M(\gamma) = \int \sqrt{\dot{\gamma}^\top M \dot{\gamma}} \, dt$; its well-posedness relies on the Lipschitz continuity of $M(x)$; and physically, a large eigenvalue of $M(x)$ in a specific direction enforces a small Euclidean edge length (refinement) in that direction, causing boundary layer meshes to be thin in the wall-normal direction where the eigenvalue is large.
notations:
- $\Omega$: A bounded subset of $\mathbb{R}^2$ representing the physical domain.
- $M(x)$: A spatially varying, symmetric, positive-definite tensor field $\Omega \to \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 2}$, referred to as the Riemannian metric.
- $\langle u, v \rangle_x$: The inner product on the tangent space at $x$, defined as $u^\top M(x) v$.
- $\|v\|_x$: The Riemannian norm of a vector $v$ at $x$, defined as $\sqrt{v^\top M(x) v}$.
- $\gamma$: A piecewise continuously differentiable curve $\gamma:[0,1]\to \Omega$ with time derivative $\dot{\gamma}$.
- $L_M(\gamma)$: The Riemannian length of the curve $\gamma$.
- $d_M(p,q)$: The geodesic distance between points $p$ and $q$ induced by $L_M$.
- $\lambda_i(x), e_i(x)$: The eigenvalues and corresponding unit eigenvectors of the matrix $M(x)$.
- **Lipschitz continuous**: A function property ensuring bounded rate of change, necessary for the stability of differential geometric constructions.
- **Anisotropic Voronoi/Delaunay**: A dual pair of geometric graph structures defined using the geodesic distance $d_M$ rather than Euclidean distance.
assumption: The domain $\Omega$ contains a boundary layer where the metric $M(x)$ is constructed to resolve strong gradients. Specifically, $M(x)$ possesses a large eigenvalue corresponding to the wall-normal direction and a small eigenvalue corresponding to the wall-tangential direction.

title: Riemannian Length Functional
content:
For a smooth manifold with a metric tensor $M(x)$, the length of a curve $\gamma$ is the integral of the local norm of its velocity vector. To ensure the length is invariant under reparameterization of the curve (geometric consistency), the functional must be homogeneous of degree 1 in velocity:
$$ L_M(\gamma) = \int_0^1 \|\dot{\gamma}(t)\|_{\gamma(t)} \, dt = \int_0^1 \sqrt{\dot{\gamma}(t)^\top M(\gamma(t)) \, \dot{\gamma}(t)} \, dt. $$
Removing the square root results in an energy functional, which depends on the parameterization speed and does not define a geometric distance.
notations:
- $\Omega$: A bounded subset of $\mathbb{R}^2$ representing the physical domain.
- $M(x)$: A spatially varying, symmetric, positive-definite tensor field $\Omega \to \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 2}$, referred to as the Riemannian metric.
- $\langle u, v \rangle_x$: The inner product on the tangent space at $x$, defined as $u^\top M(x) v$.
- $\|v\|_x$: The Riemannian norm of a vector $v$ at $x$, defined as $\sqrt{v^\top M(x) v}$.
- $\gamma$: A piecewise continuously differentiable curve $\gamma:[0,1]\to \Omega$ with time derivative $\dot{\gamma}$.
- $L_M(\gamma)$: The Riemannian length of the curve $\gamma$.
- $d_M(p,q)$: The geodesic distance between points $p$ and $q$ induced by $L_M$.
- $\lambda_i(x), e_i(x)$: The eigenvalues and corresponding unit eigenvectors of the matrix $M(x)$.
- **Lipschitz continuous**: A function property ensuring bounded rate of change, necessary for the stability of differential geometric constructions.
- **Anisotropic Voronoi/Delaunay**: A dual pair of geometric graph structures defined using the geodesic distance $d_M$ rather than Euclidean distance.

title: Well-posedness of Anisotropic Duals
content: The construction of an anisotropic Voronoi diagram based on $d_M$ and its straight-line dual (the anisotropic Delaunay triangulation) requires that the distance function $d_M$ behaves locally like a Euclidean distance. A sufficient condition for the well-posedness of these structures—ensuring bounded cells and non-degenerate duals given sufficiently dense sampling—is that the metric field $M(x)$ is uniformly Lipschitz continuous and bounded away from singularity (symmetric positive-definite).
notations:
- $\Omega$: A bounded subset of $\mathbb{R}^2$ representing the physical domain.
- $M(x)$: A spatially varying, symmetric, positive-definite tensor field $\Omega \to \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 2}$, referred to as the Riemannian metric.
- $\langle u, v \rangle_x$: The inner product on the tangent space at $x$, defined as $u^\top M(x) v$.
- $\|v\|_x$: The Riemannian norm of a vector $v$ at $x$, defined as $\sqrt{v^\top M(x) v}$.
- $\gamma$: A piecewise continuously differentiable curve $\gamma:[0,1]\to \Omega$ with time derivative $\dot{\gamma}$.
- $L_M(\gamma)$: The Riemannian length of the curve $\gamma$.
- $d_M(p,q)$: The geodesic distance between points $p$ and $q$ induced by $L_M$.
- $\lambda_i(x), e_i(x)$: The eigenvalues and corresponding unit eigenvectors of the matrix $M(x)$.
- **Lipschitz continuous**: A function property ensuring bounded rate of change, necessary for the stability of differential geometric constructions.
- **Anisotropic Voronoi/Delaunay**: A dual pair of geometric graph structures defined using the geodesic distance $d_M$ rather than Euclidean distance.

title: Eigenstructure and Physical Length Scaling
content:
In computational mesh generation, the metric $M(x)$ is defined such that edges in the resulting mesh have approximately unit length in the metric space (i.e., $d_M(p,q) \approx 1$ for adjacent vertices). Locally, for a physical displacement vector $v$ representing an edge, the condition $\|v\|_x \approx 1$ implies $v^\top M(x) v \approx 1$.
If $v$ is aligned with an eigenvector $e_i$ of $M(x)$ with eigenvalue $\lambda_i$, then the physical Euclidean length $h_i = \|v\|_{Euclidean}$ satisfies:
$$ h_i^2 \lambda_i \approx 1 \implies h_i \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda_i}}. $$
Thus, a large eigenvalue $\lambda_i$ dictates a small physical edge length (refinement) in the direction of $e_i$, and a small eigenvalue dictates a large physical edge length (stretching).
notations:
- $\Omega$: A bounded subset of $\mathbb{R}^2$ representing the physical domain.
- $M(x)$: A spatially varying, symmetric, positive-definite tensor field $\Omega \to \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 2}$, referred to as the Riemannian metric.
- $\langle u, v \rangle_x$: The inner product on the tangent space at $x$, defined as $u^\top M(x) v$.
- $\|v\|_x$: The Riemannian norm of a vector $v$ at $x$, defined as $\sqrt{v^\top M(x) v}$.
- $\gamma$: A piecewise continuously differentiable curve $\gamma:[0,1]\to \Omega$ with time derivative $\dot{\gamma}$.
- $L_M(\gamma)$: The Riemannian length of the curve $\gamma$.
- $d_M(p,q)$: The geodesic distance between points $p$ and $q$ induced by $L_M$.
- $\lambda_i(x), e_i(x)$: The eigenvalues and corresponding unit eigenvectors of the matrix $M(x)$.
- **Lipschitz continuous**: A function property ensuring bounded rate of change, necessary for the stability of differential geometric constructions.
- **Anisotropic Voronoi/Delaunay**: A dual pair of geometric graph structures defined using the geodesic distance $d_M$ rather than Euclidean distance.

title: Optimal Mesh Sizing for Residual and Adjoint Indicators
content:
For a second-order scheme ($p=2$) under the constraint of a fixed arithmetic mean size $h_{\mathrm{ref}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-2}$, the optimal mesh sizing is derived from the equidistribution principle as follows:
1. **Residual-based:** The indicator is $\eta_K = r_K$, leading to the sizing law $h_K \propto \eta_K^{-1/2}$. The resulting optimal sizes are $h_{K_1} \approx 8.90 \times 10^{-3}$, $h_{K_2} \approx 1.41 \times 10^{-2}$, and $h_{K_3} \approx 7.03 \times 10^{-3}$.
2. **Adjoint-based:** The indicator is $\zeta_K = |w_K| r_K$ (weighting the residual by adjoint sensitivity), leading to the sizing law $h_K \propto \zeta_K^{-1/2}$. The resulting optimal sizes are $h_{K_1} \approx 5.29 \times 10^{-3}$, $h_{K_2} \approx 1.45 \times 10^{-2}$, and $h_{K_3} \approx 1.02 \times 10^{-2}$.
notations:
- $\Omega$: A polygonal domain in $\mathbb{R}^2$ representing the flow field.
- $K$: A triangular control volume (cell) in the mesh.
- $h_K$: The characteristic mesh size (length scale) of cell $K$.
- $p$: The formal order of accuracy of the numerical scheme on smooth solutions.
- $r_K$: The Euclidean norm of the discrete residual vector for cell $K$.
- $w_K$: The magnitude of the adjoint solution vector sampled at the centroid of cell $K$.
- $J(\boldsymbol{u})$: A scalar quantity of interest (QoI), such as lift or drag.
- $\eta_K$: The local error indicator for residual-based adaptation.
- $\zeta_K$: The local error indicator for adjoint-based adaptation.
- $\alpha, \alpha'$: Normalization constants determined by global constraints.
- $h_{\mathrm{ref}}$: A reference target size, specifically the arithmetic mean of the local sizes.
- $\text{equidistribution}$: The strategy of refining the mesh such that a specific error density is constant across all elements.
assumption:
The system consists of three adjacent cells $K_1, K_2, K_3$ with a numerical scheme order $p=2$.
The data for these cells are:
- Residual norms: $r_{K_1} = 1.0 \times 10^{-2}$, $r_{K_2} = 4.0 \times 10^{-3}$, $r_{K_3} = 1.6 \times 10^{-2}$.
- Adjoint magnitudes: $w_{K_1} = 3$, $w_{K_2} = 1$, $w_{K_3} = 0.5$.
The global constraint requires the arithmetic mean of the target sizes to be $h_{\mathrm{ref}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-2}$.

title: Truncation Error Scaling
content: For a numerical scheme of order $p$ applied to a smooth solution on a mesh with characteristic size $h_K$, the local truncation error $\tau_K$ scales asymptotically as $\|\tau_K\| \propto h_K^p$.

title: Residual as Error Proxy
content: For a sufficiently converged primal solution, the discrete residual norm $r_K$ serves as a computable proxy for the local truncation error amplitude, implying the scaling relationship $r_K \propto h_K^p$.

title: Adjoint Error Representation
content: The error in a functional quantity of interest $J(\boldsymbol{u})$ can be approximated to first order by the inner product of the adjoint solution and the primal residual. Consequently, the local contribution to the QoI error scales as the product of the adjoint magnitude and the residual norm: $\epsilon_{K}^{\text{QoI}} \propto w_K r_K$.

title: Principle of Equidistribution
content: To minimize the global error for a fixed computational cost (or meet a tolerance with minimal cost), the mesh size $h_K$ is chosen such that the local error density is constant across all elements. If an error indicator $E_K$ scales with mesh size as $E_K \propto h_K^p$, then the equidistribution condition $E_K = \text{const}$ implies the optimal metric $h_K \propto (E_K / h_K^p)^{-1/p}$. Equivalently, if the provided indicator $I_K$ represents the unscaled error magnitude (i.e., $I_K$ is the value before scaling by $h_K$), the target size is $h_K \propto I_K^{-1/p}$.

title: Robust Geometric Predicate Evaluation Strategy
content: In 2D Delaunay triangulation, the sign of the $\operatorname{orient2d}$ predicate is invariant under uniform nonzero coordinate scaling, and robust implementation of geometric predicates is achieved by first applying adaptive filtering—accepting the sign of a floating-point approximation only when its magnitude strictly exceeds a rigorous error bound derived from the unit roundoff—and utilizing expansion arithmetic to compute exact values when the approximation is insufficient.
notations:
- $fl(\cdot)$: The result of a floating-point operation.
- $u$: The unit roundoff in the IEEE-754 floating-point standard.
- $\operatorname{orient2d}(p, q, r)$: A geometric predicate determining the orientation (counter-clockwise, clockwise, collinear) of three points $p, q, r$.
- $\operatorname{incircle}(p, q, r, s)$: A geometric predicate determining whether point $s$ lies inside, on, or outside the circumcircle of triangle $pqr$.
- **Expansion Arithmetic**: A technique representing a real number as the sum of nonoverlapping floating-point components to allow exact arithmetic operations.
- **Adaptive Filtering**: A technique that computes a floating-point approximation and an error bound, accepting the sign only if the approximation magnitude exceeds the bound.
assumption: The system is a 2D Delaunay triangulation module for mesh generation where correctness depends strictly on the robust evaluation of the signs of the $\operatorname{orient2d}$ and $\operatorname{incircle}$ predicates, and inputs may span multiple scales.

title: Definition and Expansion of the Orientation Predicate
content:
For three points $p=(x_p,y_p)$, $q=(x_q,y_q)$, and $r=(x_r,y_r)$, the orientation predicate is defined as the determinant:
$$
\operatorname{orient2d}(p,q,r) = 
\begin{vmatrix}
x_p & y_p & 1 \\
x_q & y_q & 1 \\
x_r & y_r & 1
\end{vmatrix}
$$
The algebraic expansion of this determinant is $(x_q - x_p)(y_r - y_p) - (y_q - y_p)(x_r - x_p)$. The sign of this value determines the orientation.
notations:
- $fl(\cdot)$: The result of a floating-point operation.
- $u$: The unit roundoff in the IEEE-754 floating-point standard.
- $\operatorname{orient2d}(p, q, r)$: A geometric predicate determining the orientation (counter-clockwise, clockwise, collinear) of three points $p, q, r$.
- $\operatorname{incircle}(p, q, r, s)$: A geometric predicate determining whether point $s$ lies inside, on, or outside the circumcircle of triangle $pqr$.
- **Expansion Arithmetic**: A technique representing a real number as the sum of nonoverlapping floating-point components to allow exact arithmetic operations.
- **Adaptive Filtering**: A technique that computes a floating-point approximation and an error bound, accepting the sign only if the approximation magnitude exceeds the bound.

title: Algebraic Structure of the Incircle Predicate
content:
For four points $p, q, r, s$, the incircle predicate is defined as the determinant:
$$
\operatorname{incircle}(p,q,r,s) =
\begin{vmatrix}
x_p & y_p & x_p^2 + y_p^2 & 1 \\
x_q & y_q & x_q^2 + y_q^2 & 1 \\
x_r & y_r & x_r^2 + y_r^2 & 1 \\
x_s & y_s & x_s^2 + y_s^2 & 1
\end{vmatrix}
$$
This expression depends on quadratic terms ($x^2 + y^2$), making it non-linear with respect to the coordinates.
notations:
- $fl(\cdot)$: The result of a floating-point operation.
- $u$: The unit roundoff in the IEEE-754 floating-point standard.
- $\operatorname{orient2d}(p, q, r)$: A geometric predicate determining the orientation (counter-clockwise, clockwise, collinear) of three points $p, q, r$.
- $\operatorname{incircle}(p, q, r, s)$: A geometric predicate determining whether point $s$ lies inside, on, or outside the circumcircle of triangle $pqr$.
- **Expansion Arithmetic**: A technique representing a real number as the sum of nonoverlapping floating-point components to allow exact arithmetic operations.
- **Adaptive Filtering**: A technique that computes a floating-point approximation and an error bound, accepting the sign only if the approximation magnitude exceeds the bound.

title: Floating-Point Error Bound and Adaptive Filtering Condition
content:
Under the IEEE-754 model, elementary operations satisfy $fl(a \circ b) = (a \circ b)(1+\delta)$ with $|\delta| \le u$.
Adaptive filtering computes an approximate value $A$ and a rigorous maximum error bound $B$. The true value $T$ is guaranteed to lie in the interval $[A - B, A + B]$.
The sign of $A$ is certified to be the sign of $T$ if and only if the interval does not contain zero, which corresponds to the condition $|A| > B$.
notations:
- $fl(\cdot)$: The result of a floating-point operation.
- $u$: The unit roundoff in the IEEE-754 floating-point standard.
- $\operatorname{orient2d}(p, q, r)$: A geometric predicate determining the orientation (counter-clockwise, clockwise, collinear) of three points $p, q, r$.
- $\operatorname{incircle}(p, q, r, s)$: A geometric predicate determining whether point $s$ lies inside, on, or outside the circumcircle of triangle $pqr$.
- **Expansion Arithmetic**: A technique representing a real number as the sum of nonoverlapping floating-point components to allow exact arithmetic operations.
- **Adaptive Filtering**: A technique that computes a floating-point approximation and an error bound, accepting the sign only if the approximation magnitude exceeds the bound.

title: Exactness via Expansion Arithmetic
content: Expansion arithmetic represents a real number $X$ as a sum of $m$ floating-point components $X = \sum_{i=1}^m e_i$, where the components are nonoverlapping (the least significant bit of $e_i$ is more significant than the most significant bit of $e_{i+1}$). This representation allows for the exact evaluation of polynomial expressions, such as geometric determinants, by eliminating rounding errors through error-free transformations.
notations:
- $fl(\cdot)$: The result of a floating-point operation.
- $u$: The unit roundoff in the IEEE-754 floating-point standard.
- $\operatorname{orient2d}(p, q, r)$: A geometric predicate determining the orientation (counter-clockwise, clockwise, collinear) of three points $p, q, r$.
- $\operatorname{incircle}(p, q, r, s)$: A geometric predicate determining whether point $s$ lies inside, on, or outside the circumcircle of triangle $pqr$.
- **Expansion Arithmetic**: A technique representing a real number as the sum of nonoverlapping floating-point components to allow exact arithmetic operations.
- **Adaptive Filtering**: A technique that computes a floating-point approximation and an error bound, accepting the sign only if the approximation magnitude exceeds the bound.

title: Net Interior Residual Under Orientation Error
content: Given the linear advection system with the specified Lax-Friedrichs flux and geometric parameters, if the orientation of the shared edge normal is incorrectly reused for both adjacent control volumes, the net interior contribution to the total discrete residual is exactly $-2$.
notations:
- FVM: Finite Volume Method, a method for representing and evaluating partial differential equations in the form of algebraic equations.
- $u$: The scalar conserved variable (e.g., density or concentration).
- $\boldsymbol{a}$: The constant advection velocity vector in $\mathbb{R}^2$.
- $T_L, T_R$: The left (upper) and right (lower) triangular control volumes sharing an edge.
- $e$: The shared edge between two control volumes.
- $|e|$: The length of the edge $e$.
- $\boldsymbol{n}$: The unit normal vector to an edge.
- $\phi(u_L, u_R, \boldsymbol{n})$: The numerical flux function across an edge defined by normal $\boldsymbol{n}$, given left state $u_L$ and right state $u_R$.
- $s$: The stabilization speed in the Lax-Friedrichs flux, determined by the maximum wave speed.
- $R_{\text{int}}$: The net interior residual contribution from the shared edge to the total discrete system.
assumption:
The system is defined by the following parameters:
1.  **States**: Piecewise constant states $u_L = \frac{6}{5}$ in triangle $T_L$ and $u_R = \frac{4}{5}$ in triangle $T_R$.
2.  **Advection Velocity**: $\boldsymbol{a} = (2, 1)$.
3.  **Geometry**: The shared edge $e$ has length $|e| = 1$. The defined unit normal is $\boldsymbol{n} = (0, -1)$, pointing from $T_L$ to $T_R$.
4.  **Error Condition**: While $T_L$ uses the correct outward normal $\boldsymbol{n}$ for its flux calculation, $T_R$ incorrectly uses the same vector $\boldsymbol{n}$ instead of the correct outward normal $-\boldsymbol{n}$ for its contribution.

title: Integral Form of Linear Advection
content:
For a control volume $V$ with boundary $\partial V$, the integral conservation law for the linear advection equation $\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (u\boldsymbol{a}) = 0$ is given by
$$ \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}t}\int_{V} u\,\mathrm{d}x + \int_{\partial V} (u\boldsymbol{a})\cdot \boldsymbol{n}\,\mathrm{d}s = 0, $$
where $\boldsymbol{n}$ is the outward-pointing unit normal on $\partial V$.
notations:
- FVM: Finite Volume Method, a method for representing and evaluating partial differential equations in the form of algebraic equations.
- $u$: The scalar conserved variable (e.g., density or concentration).
- $\boldsymbol{a}$: The constant advection velocity vector in $\mathbb{R}^2$.
- $T_L, T_R$: The left (upper) and right (lower) triangular control volumes sharing an edge.
- $e$: The shared edge between two control volumes.
- $|e|$: The length of the edge $e$.
- $\boldsymbol{n}$: The unit normal vector to an edge.
- $\phi(u_L, u_R, \boldsymbol{n})$: The numerical flux function across an edge defined by normal $\boldsymbol{n}$, given left state $u_L$ and right state $u_R$.
- $s$: The stabilization speed in the Lax-Friedrichs flux, determined by the maximum wave speed.
- $R_{\text{int}}$: The net interior residual contribution from the shared edge to the total discrete system.

title: Finite Volume Discretization and Residual
content: In a vertex-centered Finite Volume Method, the surface integral is approximated as a sum of numerical fluxes over edges. For a shared edge $e$ between two volumes, the contribution to the discrete residual (the rate of change of the conserved quantity) for one volume is $|e|\,\phi(u_{\text{internal}}, u_{\text{neighbor}}, \boldsymbol{n}_{\text{out}})$. The net interior residual for the union of the two volumes is the sum of their individual contributions from that shared edge.
notations:
- FVM: Finite Volume Method, a method for representing and evaluating partial differential equations in the form of algebraic equations.
- $u$: The scalar conserved variable (e.g., density or concentration).
- $\boldsymbol{a}$: The constant advection velocity vector in $\mathbb{R}^2$.
- $T_L, T_R$: The left (upper) and right (lower) triangular control volumes sharing an edge.
- $e$: The shared edge between two control volumes.
- $|e|$: The length of the edge $e$.
- $\boldsymbol{n}$: The unit normal vector to an edge.
- $\phi(u_L, u_R, \boldsymbol{n})$: The numerical flux function across an edge defined by normal $\boldsymbol{n}$, given left state $u_L$ and right state $u_R$.
- $s$: The stabilization speed in the Lax-Friedrichs flux, determined by the maximum wave speed.
- $R_{\text{int}}$: The net interior residual contribution from the shared edge to the total discrete system.

title: Local Lax-Friedrichs (Rusanov) Flux for Linear Advection
content:
The local Lax-Friedrichs numerical flux for linear advection with velocity $\boldsymbol{a}$ across an interface with normal $\boldsymbol{n}$ is defined as:
$$ \phi(u_L, u_R, \boldsymbol{n}) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ (u_L \boldsymbol{a} \cdot \boldsymbol{n}) + (u_R \boldsymbol{a} \cdot \boldsymbol{n}) - s(u_R - u_L) \right], $$
where the stabilization speed $s$ is the absolute value of the normal advection speed, $s = |\boldsymbol{a} \cdot \boldsymbol{n}|$.
notations:
- FVM: Finite Volume Method, a method for representing and evaluating partial differential equations in the form of algebraic equations.
- $u$: The scalar conserved variable (e.g., density or concentration).
- $\boldsymbol{a}$: The constant advection velocity vector in $\mathbb{R}^2$.
- $T_L, T_R$: The left (upper) and right (lower) triangular control volumes sharing an edge.
- $e$: The shared edge between two control volumes.
- $|e|$: The length of the edge $e$.
- $\boldsymbol{n}$: The unit normal vector to an edge.
- $\phi(u_L, u_R, \boldsymbol{n})$: The numerical flux function across an edge defined by normal $\boldsymbol{n}$, given left state $u_L$ and right state $u_R$.
- $s$: The stabilization speed in the Lax-Friedrichs flux, determined by the maximum wave speed.
- $R_{\text{int}}$: The net interior residual contribution from the shared edge to the total discrete system.

title: Modeled Speedup and Efficiency for Parallel Delaunay Triangulation
content:
Given the specific parameter sets for $N$, $p$, and system characteristics (bandwidth $b$, latency $L$, contention $\gamma$), and assuming the cost models for computation ($c N \log N$), interface merging ($m f_b$), and BSP communication defined above, the speedup $S$ and efficiency $E$ for the five test cases are:
1. $S \approx 13.06$, $E \approx 0.816$
2. $S \approx 10.39$, $E \approx 0.649$
3. $S \approx 10.15$, $E \approx 0.635$
4. $S = 1.0$, $E = 1.0$
5. $S \approx 62.48$, $E \approx 0.488$
(Note: Values are approximate based on the analytical model execution).
notations:
- $N$: Total number of points in the planar set.
- $p$: Number of parallel processes (partitions).
- $T_1$: Serial execution time (seconds).
- $T_p$: Parallel execution time on $p$ processes (seconds).
- $S$: Parallel speedup, defined as $T_1 / T_p$.
- $E$: Parallel efficiency, defined as $S / p$.
- $c$: Proportionality constant for serial computational cost (seconds per point-log unit).
- $m$: Dimensionless factor scaling interface retriangulation cost relative to local computation.
- $f_b$: Fraction of local points participating in boundary exchange/interface updates (decimal fraction).
- $k$: Number of global communication rounds ($k=0$ if $p=1$).
- $L$: Communication latency per round (seconds).
- $b$: Nominal per-process bandwidth in isolation (points per second).
- $b_{\text{eff}}$: Effective per-process bandwidth accounting for contention (points per second).
- $\gamma$: Network contention factor (dimensionless).
- $\log$: Natural logarithm.

title: Serial Computational Cost Model
content: The serial execution time $T_1$ for constructing a Delaunay triangulation of $N$ points scales as $O(N \log N)$ and is modeled explicitly as $T_1 = c N \log N$.
notations:
- $N$: Total number of points in the planar set.
- $p$: Number of parallel processes (partitions).
- $T_1$: Serial execution time (seconds).
- $T_p$: Parallel execution time on $p$ processes (seconds).
- $S$: Parallel speedup, defined as $T_1 / T_p$.
- $E$: Parallel efficiency, defined as $S / p$.
- $c$: Proportionality constant for serial computational cost (seconds per point-log unit).
- $m$: Dimensionless factor scaling interface retriangulation cost relative to local computation.
- $f_b$: Fraction of local points participating in boundary exchange/interface updates (decimal fraction).
- $k$: Number of global communication rounds ($k=0$ if $p=1$).
- $L$: Communication latency per round (seconds).
- $b$: Nominal per-process bandwidth in isolation (points per second).
- $b_{\text{eff}}$: Effective per-process bandwidth accounting for contention (points per second).
- $\gamma$: Network contention factor (dimensionless).
- $\log$: Natural logarithm.

title: Parallel Local Computational Cost
content: In a domain-decomposed parallel algorithm with $p$ balanced partitions, the local workload per process is $N/p$. The base local computational time $T_{\text{comp}}$ follows the same scaling law as the serial case, given by $T_{\text{comp}} = c (N/p) \log(N/p)$.
notations:
- $N$: Total number of points in the planar set.
- $p$: Number of parallel processes (partitions).
- $T_1$: Serial execution time (seconds).
- $T_p$: Parallel execution time on $p$ processes (seconds).
- $S$: Parallel speedup, defined as $T_1 / T_p$.
- $E$: Parallel efficiency, defined as $S / p$.
- $c$: Proportionality constant for serial computational cost (seconds per point-log unit).
- $m$: Dimensionless factor scaling interface retriangulation cost relative to local computation.
- $f_b$: Fraction of local points participating in boundary exchange/interface updates (decimal fraction).
- $k$: Number of global communication rounds ($k=0$ if $p=1$).
- $L$: Communication latency per round (seconds).
- $b$: Nominal per-process bandwidth in isolation (points per second).
- $b_{\text{eff}}$: Effective per-process bandwidth accounting for contention (points per second).
- $\gamma$: Network contention factor (dimensionless).
- $\log$: Natural logarithm.

title: Interface Merge Cost Model
content: Merging and retriangulation at partition interfaces incur an additional computational cost $T_{\text{merge}}$. This cost is proportional to the boundary fraction $f_b$ of the local workload and scaled by a factor $m$, modeled as $T_{\text{merge}} = c \, m \, f_b \, (N/p) \log(N/p)$.
notations:
- $N$: Total number of points in the planar set.
- $p$: Number of parallel processes (partitions).
- $T_1$: Serial execution time (seconds).
- $T_p$: Parallel execution time on $p$ processes (seconds).
- $S$: Parallel speedup, defined as $T_1 / T_p$.
- $E$: Parallel efficiency, defined as $S / p$.
- $c$: Proportionality constant for serial computational cost (seconds per point-log unit).
- $m$: Dimensionless factor scaling interface retriangulation cost relative to local computation.
- $f_b$: Fraction of local points participating in boundary exchange/interface updates (decimal fraction).
- $k$: Number of global communication rounds ($k=0$ if $p=1$).
- $L$: Communication latency per round (seconds).
- $b$: Nominal per-process bandwidth in isolation (points per second).
- $b_{\text{eff}}$: Effective per-process bandwidth accounting for contention (points per second).
- $\gamma$: Network contention factor (dimensionless).
- $\log$: Natural logarithm.

title: Network Contention Model
content: Network contention reduces the available bandwidth as the number of processes $p$ increases. The effective bandwidth $b_{\text{eff}}$ is related to the nominal bandwidth $b$ and contention factor $\gamma$ by $b_{\text{eff}} = \frac{b}{1 + \gamma (p - 1)}$.
notations:
- $N$: Total number of points in the planar set.
- $p$: Number of parallel processes (partitions).
- $T_1$: Serial execution time (seconds).
- $T_p$: Parallel execution time on $p$ processes (seconds).
- $S$: Parallel speedup, defined as $T_1 / T_p$.
- $E$: Parallel efficiency, defined as $S / p$.
- $c$: Proportionality constant for serial computational cost (seconds per point-log unit).
- $m$: Dimensionless factor scaling interface retriangulation cost relative to local computation.
- $f_b$: Fraction of local points participating in boundary exchange/interface updates (decimal fraction).
- $k$: Number of global communication rounds ($k=0$ if $p=1$).
- $L$: Communication latency per round (seconds).
- $b$: Nominal per-process bandwidth in isolation (points per second).
- $b_{\text{eff}}$: Effective per-process bandwidth accounting for contention (points per second).
- $\gamma$: Network contention factor (dimensionless).
- $\log$: Natural logarithm.

title: Communication Cost Model
content: The algorithm uses a Bulk Synchronous Parallel model with $k$ communication rounds. For $p > 1$, the total communication time $T_{\text{comm}}$ is the sum of latency and payload transmission time for all rounds. The payload per round per process is $f_b (N/p)$ points. Thus, $T_{\text{comm}} = k \left( L + \frac{f_b (N/p)}{b_{\text{eff}}} \right)$. If $p=1$, $T_{\text{comm}} = 0$.
notations:
- $N$: Total number of points in the planar set.
- $p$: Number of parallel processes (partitions).
- $T_1$: Serial execution time (seconds).
- $T_p$: Parallel execution time on $p$ processes (seconds).
- $S$: Parallel speedup, defined as $T_1 / T_p$.
- $E$: Parallel efficiency, defined as $S / p$.
- $c$: Proportionality constant for serial computational cost (seconds per point-log unit).
- $m$: Dimensionless factor scaling interface retriangulation cost relative to local computation.
- $f_b$: Fraction of local points participating in boundary exchange/interface updates (decimal fraction).
- $k$: Number of global communication rounds ($k=0$ if $p=1$).
- $L$: Communication latency per round (seconds).
- $b$: Nominal per-process bandwidth in isolation (points per second).
- $b_{\text{eff}}$: Effective per-process bandwidth accounting for contention (points per second).
- $\gamma$: Network contention factor (dimensionless).
- $\log$: Natural logarithm.

title: Total Parallel Time and Metrics
content: The total parallel execution time $T_p$ is the sum of local computation, interface merging, and communication times: $T_p = T_{\text{comp}} + T_{\text{merge}} + T_{\text{comm}}$. Speedup is defined as $S = T_1 / T_p$, and efficiency as $E = S / p$.
notations:
- $N$: Total number of points in the planar set.
- $p$: Number of parallel processes (partitions).
- $T_1$: Serial execution time (seconds).
- $T_p$: Parallel execution time on $p$ processes (seconds).
- $S$: Parallel speedup, defined as $T_1 / T_p$.
- $E$: Parallel efficiency, defined as $S / p$.
- $c$: Proportionality constant for serial computational cost (seconds per point-log unit).
- $m$: Dimensionless factor scaling interface retriangulation cost relative to local computation.
- $f_b$: Fraction of local points participating in boundary exchange/interface updates (decimal fraction).
- $k$: Number of global communication rounds ($k=0$ if $p=1$).
- $L$: Communication latency per round (seconds).
- $b$: Nominal per-process bandwidth in isolation (points per second).
- $b_{\text{eff}}$: Effective per-process bandwidth accounting for contention (points per second).
- $\gamma$: Network contention factor (dimensionless).
- $\log$: Natural logarithm.

title: Degeneracy Effects on Delaunay Triangulation
content: The Delaunay triangulation of a planar point set $P$ is non-unique if $P$ contains four cocircular points forming a convex quadrilateral (as the empty circumdisk condition is satisfied by multiple topologies), is non-existent if $P$ is fully collinear (due to the lack of non-zero area triangles), and can be rendered computationally unique in degenerate cases through the application of symbolic perturbation.
notations:
- $P$: A finite set of points in the two-dimensional Euclidean plane $\mathbb{R}^2$.
- $\text{DT}(P)$: The Delaunay Triangulation of the point set $P$.
- $\mathcal{C}(T)$: The open circumdisk of a triangle $T$, defined as the interior of the circle passing through the three vertices of $T$.
- **Cocircular**: A property of a set of points that all lie on the boundary of the same circle.
- **Collinear**: A property of a set of points that all lie on the same straight line.
- **Degeneracy**: A geometric configuration (such as cocircularity or collinearity) that causes standard geometric predicates (like the incircle test) to evaluate to zero/equality.
- **Symbolic Perturbation**: An algorithmic technique where input coordinates are treated as if perturbed by an infinitesimal amount $\epsilon$ to resolve equality in geometric predicates without altering the actual coordinate values.
assumption: Consider the point set $P_{\mathrm{sq}}=\{(0,0),(1,0),(1,1),(0,1)\}$, which forms a square with four cocircular points, and the set $P_{\mathrm{col}}=\{(0,0),(1,0),(2,0),(0,1)\}$, which contains three collinear points along the x-axis and one point off-axis.

title: Definition of Delaunay Triangulation
content: A Delaunay triangulation of a point set $P$ is a subdivision of the convex hull of $P$ into non-overlapping triangles such that for every triangle $T$ in the subdivision, the open circumdisk $\mathcal{C}(T)$ contains no points from $P$ (the empty circumdisk property).
notations:
- $P$: A finite set of points in the two-dimensional Euclidean plane $\mathbb{R}^2$.
- $\text{DT}(P)$: The Delaunay Triangulation of the point set $P$.
- $\mathcal{C}(T)$: The open circumdisk of a triangle $T$, defined as the interior of the circle passing through the three vertices of $T$.
- **Cocircular**: A property of a set of points that all lie on the boundary of the same circle.
- **Collinear**: A property of a set of points that all lie on the same straight line.
- **Degeneracy**: A geometric configuration (such as cocircularity or collinearity) that causes standard geometric predicates (like the incircle test) to evaluate to zero/equality.
- **Symbolic Perturbation**: An algorithmic technique where input coordinates are treated as if perturbed by an infinitesimal amount $\epsilon$ to resolve equality in geometric predicates without altering the actual coordinate values.

title: Condition for Planar Triangulation Existence
content: A valid triangulation in the Euclidean plane requires the formation of triangles with non-zero area; if all points in a set $P$ are collinear, the area of the convex hull is zero, and no non-degenerate triangles can be formed.
notations:
- $P$: A finite set of points in the two-dimensional Euclidean plane $\mathbb{R}^2$.
- $\text{DT}(P)$: The Delaunay Triangulation of the point set $P$.
- $\mathcal{C}(T)$: The open circumdisk of a triangle $T$, defined as the interior of the circle passing through the three vertices of $T$.
- **Cocircular**: A property of a set of points that all lie on the boundary of the same circle.
- **Collinear**: A property of a set of points that all lie on the same straight line.
- **Degeneracy**: A geometric configuration (such as cocircularity or collinearity) that causes standard geometric predicates (like the incircle test) to evaluate to zero/equality.
- **Symbolic Perturbation**: An algorithmic technique where input coordinates are treated as if perturbed by an infinitesimal amount $\epsilon$ to resolve equality in geometric predicates without altering the actual coordinate values.

title: Geometric Consequence of Cocircularity
content: If four points form a convex quadrilateral and are cocircular, the two distinct triangulations of the quadrilateral (formed by choosing either diagonal) generate triangles that share the same circumcircle. Consequently, the fourth vertex lies on the boundary (not in the interior) of the circumdisk of any triangle formed by the other three, satisfying the empty circumdisk property for both configurations.
notations:
- $P$: A finite set of points in the two-dimensional Euclidean plane $\mathbb{R}^2$.
- $\text{DT}(P)$: The Delaunay Triangulation of the point set $P$.
- $\mathcal{C}(T)$: The open circumdisk of a triangle $T$, defined as the interior of the circle passing through the three vertices of $T$.
- **Cocircular**: A property of a set of points that all lie on the boundary of the same circle.
- **Collinear**: A property of a set of points that all lie on the same straight line.
- **Degeneracy**: A geometric configuration (such as cocircularity or collinearity) that causes standard geometric predicates (like the incircle test) to evaluate to zero/equality.
- **Symbolic Perturbation**: An algorithmic technique where input coordinates are treated as if perturbed by an infinitesimal amount $\epsilon$ to resolve equality in geometric predicates without altering the actual coordinate values.

title: Role of Symbolic Perturbation
content: Symbolic perturbation resolves geometric degeneracies by replacing zero-value predicates (equalities) with consistent, deterministic non-zero values (inequalities) based on an infinitesimal logical ordering, thereby enforcing algorithmic uniqueness without altering the physical geometric constraints.
notations:
- $P$: A finite set of points in the two-dimensional Euclidean plane $\mathbb{R}^2$.
- $\text{DT}(P)$: The Delaunay Triangulation of the point set $P$.
- $\mathcal{C}(T)$: The open circumdisk of a triangle $T$, defined as the interior of the circle passing through the three vertices of $T$.
- **Cocircular**: A property of a set of points that all lie on the boundary of the same circle.
- **Collinear**: A property of a set of points that all lie on the same straight line.
- **Degeneracy**: A geometric configuration (such as cocircularity or collinearity) that causes standard geometric predicates (like the incircle test) to evaluate to zero/equality.
- **Symbolic Perturbation**: An algorithmic technique where input coordinates are treated as if perturbed by an infinitesimal amount $\epsilon$ to resolve equality in geometric predicates without altering the actual coordinate values.

title: Constraint of Partial Collinearity
content: The presence of a subset of collinear points in $P$ does not automatically preclude the existence of a unique triangulation, provided the convex hull and other points constrain the connectivity such that only one valid topology exists.
notations:
- $P$: A finite set of points in the two-dimensional Euclidean plane $\mathbb{R}^2$.
- $\text{DT}(P)$: The Delaunay Triangulation of the point set $P$.
- $\mathcal{C}(T)$: The open circumdisk of a triangle $T$, defined as the interior of the circle passing through the three vertices of $T$.
- **Cocircular**: A property of a set of points that all lie on the boundary of the same circle.
- **Collinear**: A property of a set of points that all lie on the same straight line.
- **Degeneracy**: A geometric configuration (such as cocircularity or collinearity) that causes standard geometric predicates (like the incircle test) to evaluate to zero/equality.
- **Symbolic Perturbation**: An algorithmic technique where input coordinates are treated as if perturbed by an infinitesimal amount $\epsilon$ to resolve equality in geometric predicates without altering the actual coordinate values.

title: Remeshing Trigger Time
content: Given the problem-specific flow and mesh parameters, the Delaunay remeshing trigger time $t^\star$ is uniquely determined by the logarithmic relation $t^\star = \frac{\ln(9)}{4\beta \dot{\epsilon}}$. Substituting $\beta = 0.8$ and $\dot{\epsilon} = 0.5\,\mathrm{s}^{-1}$, the value is approximately $1.373\,\mathrm{s}$.
notations:
- $t$: time variable, with $t \ge 0$.
- $\boldsymbol{x}(t)$: the coordinate vector $(x(t), y(t))$ of a mesh node at time $t$.
- $\boldsymbol{X}$: the initial coordinate vector $(X, Y)$ at $t=0$.
- $\boldsymbol{v}(\boldsymbol{x})$: the Eulerian fluid velocity field.
- $\boldsymbol{u}_m(\boldsymbol{x})$: the mesh velocity field.
- $\dot{\epsilon}$: a constant strain rate parameter for the extensional flow.
- $\beta$: a dimensionless blending factor scaling the mesh velocity.
- $L_0$: the initial side length of the equilateral triangle.
- $A(t)$: the area of the triangle at time $t$.
- $s_{ij}(t)$: the Euclidean length of the edge connecting vertex $i$ and vertex $j$ at time $t$.
- $s_{\min}(t)$: the minimum edge length of the triangle at time $t$.
- $R(t)$: the circumradius of the triangle at time $t$.
- $\rho(t)$: the radius–edge ratio quality metric, defined as $R(t)/s_{\min}(t)$.
- $\theta_{\min}(t)$: the smallest internal angle of the triangle at time $t$.
- $\theta_{\min,\mathrm{crit}}$: the critical minimum angle threshold for remeshing.
- $\rho_{\mathrm{crit}}$: the critical radius–edge ratio threshold corresponding to $\theta_{\min,\mathrm{crit}}$.
- $t^\star$: the specific time at which remeshing is triggered.
assumption:
1. The fluid velocity is a planar extensional flow: $\boldsymbol{v}(\boldsymbol{x}) = (\dot{\epsilon} x, -\dot{\epsilon} y)^T$.
2. The mesh velocity is proportional to the fluid velocity: $\boldsymbol{u}_m = \beta \boldsymbol{v}$.
3. The initial mesh element is an equilateral triangle with side length $L_0$ and vertices at $\boldsymbol{X}_1=(0,0)$, $\boldsymbol{X}_2=(L_0,0)$, and $\boldsymbol{X}_3=(L_0/2, \sqrt{3}L_0/2)$.
4. Remeshing is triggered at the smallest time $t^\star > 0$ when the quality metric $\rho(t)$ reaches the critical value $\rho_{\mathrm{crit}}$ corresponding to $\theta_{\min,\mathrm{crit}} = 30^\circ$.
5. The constants are $\dot{\epsilon} = 0.5\,\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ and $\beta = 0.8$.

title: Lagrangian Mesh Motion
content: The motion of a mesh vertex $\boldsymbol{x}(t)$ in a Lagrangian framework is governed by the first-order ordinary differential equation $\frac{d\boldsymbol{x}}{dt} = \boldsymbol{u}_m(\boldsymbol{x}, t)$, with initial condition $\boldsymbol{x}(0) = \boldsymbol{X}$.
notations:
- $t$: time variable, with $t \ge 0$.
- $\boldsymbol{x}(t)$: the coordinate vector $(x(t), y(t))$ of a mesh node at time $t$.
- $\boldsymbol{X}$: the initial coordinate vector $(X, Y)$ at $t=0$.
- $\boldsymbol{v}(\boldsymbol{x})$: the Eulerian fluid velocity field.
- $\boldsymbol{u}_m(\boldsymbol{x})$: the mesh velocity field.
- $\dot{\epsilon}$: a constant strain rate parameter for the extensional flow.
- $\beta$: a dimensionless blending factor scaling the mesh velocity.
- $L_0$: the initial side length of the equilateral triangle.
- $A(t)$: the area of the triangle at time $t$.
- $s_{ij}(t)$: the Euclidean length of the edge connecting vertex $i$ and vertex $j$ at time $t$.
- $s_{\min}(t)$: the minimum edge length of the triangle at time $t$.
- $R(t)$: the circumradius of the triangle at time $t$.
- $\rho(t)$: the radius–edge ratio quality metric, defined as $R(t)/s_{\min}(t)$.
- $\theta_{\min}(t)$: the smallest internal angle of the triangle at time $t$.
- $\theta_{\min,\mathrm{crit}}$: the critical minimum angle threshold for remeshing.
- $\rho_{\mathrm{crit}}$: the critical radius–edge ratio threshold corresponding to $\theta_{\min,\mathrm{crit}}$.
- $t^\star$: the specific time at which remeshing is triggered.

title: Euclidean Distance and Triangle Properties
content: For a triangle in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with vertices $\boldsymbol{x}_1, \boldsymbol{x}_2, \boldsymbol{x}_3$, the side lengths are the Euclidean distances $s_{ij} = \|\boldsymbol{x}_i - \boldsymbol{x}_j\|$, and the area is given by the determinant formula $A = \frac{1}{2} |x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2)|$.
notations:
- $t$: time variable, with $t \ge 0$.
- $\boldsymbol{x}(t)$: the coordinate vector $(x(t), y(t))$ of a mesh node at time $t$.
- $\boldsymbol{X}$: the initial coordinate vector $(X, Y)$ at $t=0$.
- $\boldsymbol{v}(\boldsymbol{x})$: the Eulerian fluid velocity field.
- $\boldsymbol{u}_m(\boldsymbol{x})$: the mesh velocity field.
- $\dot{\epsilon}$: a constant strain rate parameter for the extensional flow.
- $\beta$: a dimensionless blending factor scaling the mesh velocity.
- $L_0$: the initial side length of the equilateral triangle.
- $A(t)$: the area of the triangle at time $t$.
- $s_{ij}(t)$: the Euclidean length of the edge connecting vertex $i$ and vertex $j$ at time $t$.
- $s_{\min}(t)$: the minimum edge length of the triangle at time $t$.
- $R(t)$: the circumradius of the triangle at time $t$.
- $\rho(t)$: the radius–edge ratio quality metric, defined as $R(t)/s_{\min}(t)$.
- $\theta_{\min}(t)$: the smallest internal angle of the triangle at time $t$.
- $\theta_{\min,\mathrm{crit}}$: the critical minimum angle threshold for remeshing.
- $\rho_{\mathrm{crit}}$: the critical radius–edge ratio threshold corresponding to $\theta_{\min,\mathrm{crit}}$.
- $t^\star$: the specific time at which remeshing is triggered.

title: Circumradius and Radius-Edge Ratio
content: The circumradius $R$ of a triangle with side lengths $a, b, c$ and area $A$ is given by $R = \frac{abc}{4A}$. The radius–edge ratio quality metric is defined as $\rho = \frac{R}{s_{\min}}$, where $s_{\min} = \min(a, b, c)$.
notations:
- $t$: time variable, with $t \ge 0$.
- $\boldsymbol{x}(t)$: the coordinate vector $(x(t), y(t))$ of a mesh node at time $t$.
- $\boldsymbol{X}$: the initial coordinate vector $(X, Y)$ at $t=0$.
- $\boldsymbol{v}(\boldsymbol{x})$: the Eulerian fluid velocity field.
- $\boldsymbol{u}_m(\boldsymbol{x})$: the mesh velocity field.
- $\dot{\epsilon}$: a constant strain rate parameter for the extensional flow.
- $\beta$: a dimensionless blending factor scaling the mesh velocity.
- $L_0$: the initial side length of the equilateral triangle.
- $A(t)$: the area of the triangle at time $t$.
- $s_{ij}(t)$: the Euclidean length of the edge connecting vertex $i$ and vertex $j$ at time $t$.
- $s_{\min}(t)$: the minimum edge length of the triangle at time $t$.
- $R(t)$: the circumradius of the triangle at time $t$.
- $\rho(t)$: the radius–edge ratio quality metric, defined as $R(t)/s_{\min}(t)$.
- $\theta_{\min}(t)$: the smallest internal angle of the triangle at time $t$.
- $\theta_{\min,\mathrm{crit}}$: the critical minimum angle threshold for remeshing.
- $\rho_{\mathrm{crit}}$: the critical radius–edge ratio threshold corresponding to $\theta_{\min,\mathrm{crit}}$.
- $t^\star$: the specific time at which remeshing is triggered.

title: Relation Between Radius-Edge Ratio and Minimum Angle
content: For any triangle, the ratio of the circumradius $R$ to the shortest side $s_{\min}$ is related to the smallest internal angle $\theta_{\min}$ by the identity $\frac{R}{s_{\min}} = \frac{1}{2\sin(\theta_{\min})}$. Consequently, a lower bound on the minimum angle $\theta_{\min} \ge \theta_{\mathrm{crit}}$ corresponds to an upper bound on the radius–edge ratio $\rho \le \rho_{\mathrm{crit}}$, where $\rho_{\mathrm{crit}} = \frac{1}{2\sin(\theta_{\mathrm{crit}})}$.
notations:
- $t$: time variable, with $t \ge 0$.

- $\boldsymbol{v}(\boldsymbol{x})$: the Eulerian fluid velocity field.
- $\boldsymbol{u}_m(\boldsymbol{x})$: the mesh velocity field.

- $s_{\min}(t)$: the minimum edge length of the triangle at time $t$.
- $R(t)$: the circumradius of the triangle at time $t$.

- $\theta_{\min}(t)$: the smallest internal angle of the triangle at time $t$.
- $\theta_{\min,\mathrm{crit}}$: the critical minimum angle threshold for remeshing.

title: Characterization of Delaunay Triangulation
content:
The Delaunay graph $\mathcal{D}(P)$ is the geometric dual of the Voronoi diagram of $P$, where edges connect sites sharing a Voronoi edge.
1.  **General Position:** If no 4 sites are cocircular, $\mathcal{D}(P)$ is a unique triangulation where every triangle $\triangle(p_i, p_j, p_k)$ has a circumcircle whose open disk contains no site of $P$.
2.  **Degeneracy:** If 4 sites are cocircular, the Voronoi diagram has a vertex of degree 4, corresponding to a convex quadrilateral in $\mathcal{D}(P)$. A triangulation is obtained by choosing either diagonal, both satisfying the empty circumcircle condition.
3.  **Convex Hull:** Edges of the convex hull of $P$ correspond to unbounded Voronoi edges.
notations:
- $P$: A finite set of discrete points (sites) in $\mathbb{R}^2$.
- $\|\cdot\|$: The standard Euclidean norm in $\mathbb{R}^2$.
- $V(p)$: The Voronoi cell of a site $p \in P$, defined as the set of points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ closer to or equidistant to $p$ than to any other site in $P$.
- $\mathcal{D}(P)$: The Delaunay graph (or triangulation) of the set $P$.
- $B(c, r)$: The open disk in $\mathbb{R}^2$ centered at $c$ with radius $r$, defined as $\{ x \in \mathbb{R}^2 : \|x - c\| < r \}$.
- $\triangle(p_i, p_j, p_k)$: The triangle formed by vertices $p_i, p_j, p_k$.
- **General Position**: A configuration of $P$ where no three points are collinear and no four points are cocircular.
- **Degeneracy**: A configuration where geometric coincidences occur, specifically four or more sites lying on the same circle.
assumption: The domain is $\mathbb{R}^2$ equipped with the Euclidean metric. The set $P$ is finite. We consider both the case of general position (no 4 cocircular points) and degenerate cases (4 or more cocircular points).

title: Definition of Voronoi Cell
content: For a set of sites $P \subset \mathbb{R}^2$, the Voronoi cell for a specific site $p \in P$ is defined as $V(p) = \{ x \in \mathbb{R}^2 : \|x - p\| \le \|x - q\| \text{ for all } q \in P \}$.

- $\mathcal{D}(P)$: The Delaunay graph (or triangulation) of the set $P$.

- **Degeneracy**: A configuration where geometric coincidences occur, specifically four or more sites lying on the same circle.

title: Voronoi Features Definition
content: A Voronoi edge is the nonempty intersection of exactly two Voronoi cells, consisting of points equidistant to the two corresponding sites and strictly closer to them than to any other site. A Voronoi vertex is the intersection of at least three Voronoi cells, consisting of a point equidistant to the corresponding sites and strictly closer to them than to any other site.

- $P$: A finite set of discrete points (sites) in $\mathbb{R}^2$.

title: Definition of Delaunay Graph via Duality
content: The Delaunay graph $\mathcal{D}(P)$ is the geometric dual of the Voronoi diagram. An edge connects two sites $p_i, p_j \in P$ in $\mathcal{D}(P)$ if and only if their Voronoi cells $V(p_i)$ and $V(p_j)$ share a common Voronoi edge.

title: Geometric Property of Circumcenters
content: For any three non-collinear points $p_i, p_j, p_k$, there exists a unique circumcircle passing through them. The center $c$ of this circle satisfies $\|c - p_i\| = \|c - p_j\| = \|c - p_k\| = r$.

title: Relation between Voronoi Vertices and Circumcircles
content: A point $c$ is a Voronoi vertex incident to cells $V(p_i), V(p_j), V(p_k)$ if and only if $c$ is the center of a circle passing through $p_i, p_j, p_k$ such that no other site $q \in P$ lies strictly inside the circle (i.e., $\|c - q\| \ge r$ for all $q \in P$).

title: Correct Properties of Regular Triangulation in Mesh Adaptation
content: In the context of isotropic mesh adaptation, selecting vertex weights $w_i$ that increase monotonically with the local target length scale $h(x_i)$ promotes the equidistribution of interpolation error for piecewise linear elements. Furthermore, the resulting regular triangulation is geometrically characterized by the empty-power-ball property, where for each triangle there exists a center $c$ and parameter $r$ such that $\|p_i - c\|^2 - w_i^2 = r^2$ for the vertices and is strictly greater for all other points.
notations:
- $\Omega$: The computational domain, a subset of $\mathbb{R}^2$.
- $p(x)$: A scalar field representing pressure defined on $\Omega$.
- $H_p(x)$: The Hessian matrix of the pressure field at point $x$, containing second-order partial derivatives.
- $\Pi p$: The piecewise linear interpolant of the pressure field on a triangulation.
- $h_T$: A characteristic length scale (e.g., edge length) of a triangle $T$.
- $h(x)$: A scalar target length scale function defined over $\Omega$.
- $w_i$: A scalar weight associated with a vertex $p_i$.
- $\pi_w(x, p_i)$: The power distance between a point $x$ and a weighted point $p_i$, defined as $\|x - p_i\|^2 - w_i^2$.
- **Regular Triangulation**: Also known as Weighted Delaunay Triangulation, the geometric dual of the Power Diagram (Laguerre Voronoi diagram).
assumption: The mesh adaptation process defines the vertex weights $w_i$ as a strictly increasing function of the local target length scale $h(x_i)$, i.e., $w_i = \phi(h(x_i))$ where $\phi' > 0$.

title: Linear Interpolation Error Bound
content: For piecewise linear finite elements on a mesh with characteristic element size $h$, the local interpolation error in the $L^\infty$ norm is bounded by the product of the Hessian norm and the square of the element size: $\|p - \Pi p\| \le C \max \|H_p\| h^2$.

- $\Pi p$: The piecewise linear interpolant of the pressure field on a triangulation.
- $h_T$: A characteristic length scale (e.g., edge length) of a triangle $T$.
- $h(x)$: A scalar target length scale function defined over $\Omega$.
- $w_i$: A scalar weight associated with a vertex $p_i$.
- $\pi_w(x, p_i)$: The power distance between a point $x$ and a weighted point $p_i$, defined as $\|x - p_i\|^2 - w_i^2$.
- **Regular Triangulation**: Also known as Weighted Delaunay Triangulation, the geometric dual of the Power Diagram (Laguerre Voronoi diagram).

title: Equidistribution Principle
content: An optimal isotropic mesh adaptation strategy seeks to distribute the local error uniformly across the domain. Based on the error bound, this implies the target length scale $h(x)$ should be inversely proportional to the square root of the Hessian norm: $h(x) \propto \|H_p(x)\|^{-1/2}$.

title: Geometric Property of Power Diagrams
content: In a Power Diagram (and its dual Regular Triangulation), increasing the weight $w_i$ of a generator $p_i$ relative to its neighbors increases the volume of its corresponding Voronoi cell. Consequently, the local mesh element size in the dual triangulation is positively correlated with the magnitude of the local weights.

title: Empty Power Ball Property
content: A set of vertices in $\mathbb{R}^d$ forms a simplex in the Regular Triangulation if and only if there exists a point $c$ (the power center) and a squared radius $r^2$ such that the power distance from $c$ to each vertex of the simplex is equal to $r^2$, and the power distance from $c$ to any other site in the point set is strictly greater than $r^2$.

title: Scalar vs. Tensor Isotropy
content: A scalar weight field provides a single degree of freedom per point, allowing for the control of local density (isotropic scaling) but lacking the directional information required to define anisotropy (stretching along specific axes). Anisotropy requires a metric tensor field.

title: Weight Dependence in Power Distance
content: The power distance function $\pi_w(x, p_i) = \|x - p_i\|^2 - w_i^2$ depends on the square of the weight $w_i$. Therefore, the sign of $w_i$ does not influence the geometric structure of the triangulation.

title: Affine Variance of Euclidean Distance
content: Geometric structures defined by Euclidean distance conditions (such as spheres or power distances) are invariant under Euclidean isometries but not under general affine transformations, which distort spheres into ellipsoids.

title: Valid Properties of 3D Delaunay Tetrahedralization
content: For a set of points $P \subset \mathbb{R}^3$, the Delaunay tetrahedralization is correctly defined as the set of tetrahedra whose circumspheres are empty of other sites in $P$, a property strictly dual to the condition that the Voronoi cells of the tetrahedron's vertices meet at a common Voronoi vertex. Furthermore, while the 2D Delaunay triangulation becomes non-unique with 4 co-circular sites, the 3D Delaunay tetrahedralization becomes non-unique only when at least 5 sites are co-spherical.
notations:
- $P$: A finite set of discrete points (sites) in Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^d$.
- $DT(P)$: The Delaunay triangulation (in 2D) or tetrahedralization (in 3D) of the set $P$.
- $V(p)$: The Voronoi cell of a site $p \in P$, defined as the set of points in $\mathbb{R}^d$ closer to $p$ than to any other site in $P$.
- Simplex: The generalization of a triangle or tetrahedron to arbitrary dimensions (triangle in 2D, tetrahedron in 3D).
- Circumhypersphere: The unique $(d-1)$-sphere passing through the $d+1$ vertices of a non-degenerate simplex in $\mathbb{R}^d$.
- General Position: A configuration of points $P$ where no subset of size $d+2$ is co-hyperspherical (co-circular in 2D, co-spherical in 3D).
assumption: The system is defined for a finite set of sites $P$ in $\mathbb{R}^3$ (3D space), compared against properties in $\mathbb{R}^2$ (2D space), utilizing the standard Euclidean $L_2$ metric.

title: Voronoi-Delaunay Duality
content: In $\mathbb{R}^d$, a simplex with vertices $\{p_1, \dots, p_{d+1}\} \subset P$ belongs to $DT(P)$ if and only if the intersection of the Voronoi cells $V(p_1) \cap \dots \cap V(p_{d+1})$ is non-empty. This intersection is a Voronoi vertex, which is a point equidistant to all $d+1$ sites and closer to no other site in $P$.

- General Position: A configuration of points $P$ where no subset of size $d+2$ is co-hyperspherical (co-circular in 2D, co-spherical in 3D).

title: Empty Circumhypersphere Property
content: A simplex defined by $d+1$ points in $P$ is a face of $DT(P)$ if and only if its circumscribing hypersphere contains no other site of $P$ in its interior.

title: Uniqueness and Degeneracy Conditions
content: The Delaunay structure $DT(P)$ is unique if and only if the points are in general position. Non-uniqueness arises precisely when $d+2$ sites lie on the same empty circumhypersphere, allowing the enclosed convex polytope to be triangulated in multiple ways. In 2D, this threshold is 4 co-circular sites; in 3D, it is 5 co-spherical sites.

title: Optimality Divergence in Higher Dimensions
content: In 2D, the Delaunay triangulation maximizes the minimum internal angle of the triangles. In 3D, the Delaunay tetrahedralization does **not** maximize the minimum dihedral angle, as it can contain "slivers" (tetrahedra with small volumes and small dihedral angles) even when the triangulation is unique and satisfies the empty circumsphere property.

title: Geometric and Numerical Properties of Radius-Edge Ratio in Mesh Generation
content:
Based on the geometric definitions and linear finite element analysis:
1.  **Exact 2D Relation:** For any triangle $\Delta$, the radius-edge ratio is exactly determined by the minimum angle: $\rho_{\Delta} = \frac{1}{2 \sin \theta_{\min}}$.
2.  **2D Mesh Quality:** Enforcing an upper bound $\rho_{\Delta} \le \bar{\rho}$ guarantees a minimum angle $\theta_{\min} \ge \arcsin(\frac{1}{2 \bar{\rho}})$.
3.  **Numerical Stability:** For triangular linear elements, bounding $\rho_{\Delta}$ bounds the spectral condition number of the stiffness matrix, which scales as $\csc^2 \theta_{\min}$.
4.  **3D Limitation:** In three dimensions, bounding $\rho_{T}$ does **not** prevent degenerate sliver elements with arbitrarily small dihedral angles; thus, it implies neither a lower bound on dihedral angles nor guarantees good matrix conditioning without supplemental criteria.
notations:
- $\Delta$: A non-degenerate triangle in Euclidean space.
- $T$: A non-degenerate tetrahedron in Euclidean space.
- $R_{\Delta}$: The radius of the circumscribed circle of a triangle $\Delta$.
- $\ell_{\min}$: The length of the shortest edge of a mesh element (triangle or tetrahedron).
- $\rho_{\Delta}$: The radius-edge ratio of a triangle, defined as $\rho_{\Delta} = R_{\Delta} / \ell_{\min}$.
- $\rho_{T}$: The radius-edge ratio of a tetrahedron, defined as $\rho_{T} = R_{T} / \ell_{\min}$, where $R_T$ is the circumradius.
- $\theta_{\min}$: The smallest interior angle of a triangle $\Delta$.
- $\kappa(A)$: The spectral condition number of a matrix $A$, defined as the ratio of its largest to smallest singular values.
- $K_e$: The element stiffness matrix for a linear finite element discretization of an isotropic diffusion operator.
- $J$: The Jacobian matrix of the affine mapping from a reference element to the physical element.
- sliver: A type of tetrahedron with bounded radius-edge ratio but arbitrarily small dihedral angles (near-coplanar vertices).
assumption:
The mesh generation process uses Delaunay refinement.
- In 2D, the refinement enforces a global upper bound on the radius-edge ratio: $\rho_{\Delta} \le \bar{\rho}$ for some constant $\bar{\rho}$.
- In 3D, the refinement enforces a global upper bound on the radius-edge ratio: $\rho_{T} \le \bar{\rho}$.

title: Law of Sines and geometric ordering
content: For any triangle with side lengths $a, b, c$ opposite to interior angles $A, B, C$ respectively, and circumradius $R$, the relation $\frac{a}{\sin A} = \frac{b}{\sin B} = \frac{c}{\sin C} = 2R$ holds. Furthermore, the ordering of side lengths corresponds monotonically to the ordering of the opposite angles (e.g., the shortest side is opposite the smallest angle).

title: Condition number of linear element stiffness matrix
content: For a linear finite element discretizing an isotropic diffusion operator, the spectral condition number of the element stiffness matrix $K_e$ satisfies $\kappa(K_e) = (\kappa(J))^2$, where $J$ is the Jacobian of the affine map from the reference element.

title: Condition number of triangular Jacobian
content: For a triangular element with two edges of lengths $a$ and $b$ forming an angle $\theta$, the spectral condition number of the Jacobian $J$ behaves asymptotically as $\kappa(J) \asymp \frac{\max(a,b)}{\min(a,b) \sin\theta}$ as $\theta \to 0$. If the edge lengths are comparable (their ratio is bounded), then $\kappa(J) \asymp \csc \theta$.

title: Existence of sliver tetrahedra
content: In three dimensions, there exists a class of tetrahedra called "slivers" (nearly coplanar vertices distributed on a sphere) which have a bounded radius-edge ratio $\rho_T$ but possess dihedral angles arbitrarily close to $0$ and $\pi$.

title: Necessary Operations for Curved Mesh Conformity
content:
To maintain exact geometric conformity of a mesh to a curved boundary $\partial \Omega$, the following mathematical operations are necessary and sufficient conditions derived from geometry:
1. For implicit boundaries $f(\mathbf{x})=0$, boundary vertices are computed by constrained projection satisfying $\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{p} \parallel \nabla f(\mathbf{x})$ with $f(\mathbf{x})=0$, and boundary edge intersections are computed by finding roots of $f(\mathbf{e}(t)) = 0$.
2. For parametric boundaries $\mathbf{S}(u,v)$, boundary vertices are computed by solving the normal equations $\left[\partial_u \mathbf{S}, \partial_v \mathbf{S}\right]^\top (\mathbf{S}(u,v) - \mathbf{p}) = \mathbf{0}$, and boundary edge intersections are computed by solving the system $\mathbf{S}(u,v) = \mathbf{e}(t)$.
notations:
- $\mathbb{R}^3$: Three-dimensional Euclidean space.
- $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^3$: A spatial coordinate vector.
- $\Omega$: The spatial domain of interest (e.g., the fluid domain).
- $\partial \Omega$: The boundary of the domain $\Omega$.
- $f: \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}$: A scalar level-set function defining the boundary implicitly by the equation $f(\mathbf{x}) = 0$.
- $\mathbf{S}: D \subset \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^3$: A vector-valued function parameterizing the boundary surface with parameters $(u, v)$.
- $\nabla f(\mathbf{x})$: The gradient vector of $f$ at $\mathbf{x}$, representing the surface normal direction for an implicit surface.
- $\partial_u \mathbf{S}, \partial_v \mathbf{S}$: The partial derivative vectors of $\mathbf{S}$ with respect to $u$ and $v$, spanning the tangent plane.
- $\mathbf{p} \in \mathbb{R}^3$: A query point in ambient space (e.g., a candidate mesh vertex).
- $\mathbf{e}(t)$: A parametric representation of a line segment, $\mathbf{e}(t) = \mathbf{a} + t(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{a})$ for $t \in [0, 1]$.
- **KKT conditions**: Karush–Kuhn–Tucker conditions, first-order necessary conditions for a solution in nonlinear programming.
assumption: For a mesh to be geometrically conformal to a curved boundary $\partial \Omega$, all boundary vertices must lie exactly on $\partial \Omega$ and all boundary edges must be curves or segments that do not deviate from the boundary topology (e.g., segments must be split at intersections with the boundary to prevent crossing).

title: Constrained Projection onto Implicit Surfaces
content: The orthogonal projection of a point $\mathbf{p}$ onto a surface defined by the level set $f(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ is the solution to the constrained minimization problem $\min_{\mathbf{x}} \frac{1}{2}\|\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{p}\|^2$ subject to $f(\mathbf{x}) = 0$. The first-order optimality conditions (KKT conditions) for this problem imply that the displacement vector $\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{p}$ must be collinear with the gradient $\nabla f(\mathbf{x})$ (the surface normal).
notations:
- $\mathbb{R}^3$: Three-dimensional Euclidean space.
- $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^3$: A spatial coordinate vector.
- $\Omega$: The spatial domain of interest (e.g., the fluid domain).
- $\partial \Omega$: The boundary of the domain $\Omega$.
- $f: \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}$: A scalar level-set function defining the boundary implicitly by the equation $f(\mathbf{x}) = 0$.
- $\mathbf{S}: D \subset \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^3$: A vector-valued function parameterizing the boundary surface with parameters $(u, v)$.
- $\nabla f(\mathbf{x})$: The gradient vector of $f$ at $\mathbf{x}$, representing the surface normal direction for an implicit surface.
- $\partial_u \mathbf{S}, \partial_v \mathbf{S}$: The partial derivative vectors of $\mathbf{S}$ with respect to $u$ and $v$, spanning the tangent plane.
- $\mathbf{p} \in \mathbb{R}^3$: A query point in ambient space (e.g., a candidate mesh vertex).
- $\mathbf{e}(t)$: A parametric representation of a line segment, $\mathbf{e}(t) = \mathbf{a} + t(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{a})$ for $t \in [0, 1]$.
- **KKT conditions**: Karush–Kuhn–Tucker conditions, first-order necessary conditions for a solution in nonlinear programming.

title: Intersection of Line Segment and Implicit Surface
content: The intersection point(s) of a line segment $\mathbf{e}(t)$ with an implicit surface $f(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ correspond to the roots $t^*$ of the composite scalar function $g(t) = f(\mathbf{e}(t)) = 0$. The existence of such roots can be guaranteed by the Intermediate Value Theorem if the function values at the endpoints have opposite signs.
notations:
- $\mathbb{R}^3$: Three-dimensional Euclidean space.
- $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^3$: A spatial coordinate vector.
- $\Omega$: The spatial domain of interest (e.g., the fluid domain).
- $\partial \Omega$: The boundary of the domain $\Omega$.
- $f: \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}$: A scalar level-set function defining the boundary implicitly by the equation $f(\mathbf{x}) = 0$.

title: Orthogonal Projection onto Parametric Surfaces
content:
The orthogonal projection of a point $\mathbf{p}$ onto a parametric surface $\mathbf{S}(u,v)$ is found by minimizing the squared distance function $D(u,v) = \frac{1}{2}\|\mathbf{S}(u,v) - \mathbf{p}\|^2$. The necessary first-order condition is that the residual vector $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{S}(u,v) - \mathbf{p}$ is orthogonal to the surface tangent plane, satisfying the normal equations:
$$ \left[\partial_u \mathbf{S}(u,v) \cdot (\mathbf{S}(u,v) - \mathbf{p})\right] = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \left[\partial_v \mathbf{S}(u,v) \cdot (\mathbf{S}(u,v) - \mathbf{p})\right] = 0 $$
notations:
- $\mathbb{R}^3$: Three-dimensional Euclidean space.
- $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^3$: A spatial coordinate vector.
- $\Omega$: The spatial domain of interest (e.g., the fluid domain).
- $\partial \Omega$: The boundary of the domain $\Omega$.
- $f: \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}$: A scalar level-set function defining the boundary implicitly by the equation $f(\mathbf{x}) = 0$.
- $\mathbf{S}: D \subset \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^3$: A vector-valued function parameterizing the boundary surface with parameters $(u, v)$.
- $\nabla f(\mathbf{x})$: The gradient vector of $f$ at $\mathbf{x}$, representing the surface normal direction for an implicit surface.
- $\partial_u \mathbf{S}, \partial_v \mathbf{S}$: The partial derivative vectors of $\mathbf{S}$ with respect to $u$ and $v$, spanning the tangent plane.
- $\mathbf{p} \in \mathbb{R}^3$: A query point in ambient space (e.g., a candidate mesh vertex).
- $\mathbf{e}(t)$: A parametric representation of a line segment, $\mathbf{e}(t) = \mathbf{a} + t(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{a})$ for $t \in [0, 1]$.
- **KKT conditions**: Karush–Kuhn–Tucker conditions, first-order necessary conditions for a solution in nonlinear programming.

title: Intersection of Line Segment and Parametric Surface
content: The intersection of a line segment $\mathbf{e}(t)$ and a parametric surface $\mathbf{S}(u,v)$ is determined by the set of parameters $(u, v, t)$ that satisfy the vector equation $\mathbf{S}(u,v) = \mathbf{e}(t)$, which constitutes a system of three nonlinear equations in three unknowns.
notations:
- $\mathbb{R}^3$: Three-dimensional Euclidean space.
- $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^3$: A spatial coordinate vector.
- $\Omega$: The spatial domain of interest (e.g., the fluid domain).
- $\partial \Omega$: The boundary of the domain $\Omega$.
- $f: \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}$: A scalar level-set function defining the boundary implicitly by the equation $f(\mathbf{x}) = 0$.

- $\partial_u \mathbf{S}, \partial_v \mathbf{S}$: The partial derivative vectors of $\mathbf{S}$ with respect to $u$ and $v$, spanning the tangent plane.
- $\mathbf{p} \in \mathbb{R}^3$: A query point in ambient space (e.g., a candidate mesh vertex).

title: Feasibility of Hybrid Delaunay Meshing for Viscous Flows
content: For turbulent boundary layer simulations requiring $y^+ \approx 1$, the resulting mesh aspect ratio ($AR \approx 200$) renders pure Euclidean Delaunay triangulation unsuitable due to its inherent isotropy. A scientifically valid strategy requires a hybrid approach: explicitly extruded prismatic layers to resolve the wall-normal gradients, coupled to a tetrahedral core via pyramids to ensure topological conformity, with additional refinement steps (such as weighted Delaunay or restricted topological flips) required to suppress sliver tetrahedra in the 3D volume.
notations:
- $Re_x$: Local Reynolds number based on streamwise distance $x$, defined as $Re_x = U_{\infty} x / \nu$.
- $C_f$: Local skin-friction coefficient, defined as $C_f = 2\tau_w / (\rho U_{\infty}^2)$.
- $u_{\tau}$: Friction velocity, defined as $u_{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_w/\rho}$.
- $y^+$: Non-dimensional wall distance, defined as $y^+ = u_{\tau} \Delta n_1 / \nu$ for the first cell height $\Delta n_1$.
- $\tau_w$: Wall shear stress.
- $\rho$: Fluid density.
- $\nu$: Kinematic viscosity.
- $U_{\infty}$: Free-stream velocity.
- $\Delta n_1$: Height of the first mesh cell adjacent to the wall in the wall-normal direction.
- $\Delta s$: Target mesh spacing in the streamwise direction.
- $AR$: Aspect Ratio of a mesh cell, defined here as the ratio of streamwise spacing to wall-normal height, $AR = \Delta s / \Delta n_1$.
- $\delta(x)$: Boundary layer thickness at location $x$.
- **Delaunay Triangulation**: A tessellation of a point set where no point is inside the circumhypersphere of any simplex (triangle in 2D, tetrahedron in 3D).
- **Sliver**: A degenerate tetrahedron in 3D Delaunay triangulation with near-zero volume and dihedral angles, despite having a well-behaved circumradius.
- **PLC**: Piecewise Linear Complex, a set of polyhedral features (points, segments, facets) used as constraints in mesh generation.
assumption: The flow is defined by a chord length $L=1.0\ \mathrm{m}$, free stream velocity $U_{\infty}=70\ \mathrm{m/s}$, and kinematic viscosity $\nu=1.5\times 10^{-5}\ \mathrm{m^2/s}$. The analysis is performed at $x=0.20\ \mathrm{m}$. The meshing requirements are strict: the first cell height $\Delta n_1$ must satisfy $y^+ = 1$, and the streamwise spacing is fixed at $\Delta s = 1.0\times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{m}$.

title: Turbulent Boundary Layer Scaling
content: For a smooth, zero-pressure-gradient turbulent boundary layer, the local skin-friction coefficient $C_f$ and boundary layer thickness $\delta$ follow empirical power-law correlations dependent on the local Reynolds number $Re_x$. Specifically, $C_f \propto Re_x^{-1/7}$ and $\delta \propto x Re_x^{-1/5}$.

title: Geometric Property of Euclidean Delaunay Triangulation
content: In Euclidean space, Delaunay triangulation maximizes the minimum angle of triangles only in two dimensions. In three dimensions, it satisfies the empty circumsphere property but does **not** maximize the minimum solid or dihedral angle. Consequently, 3D Delaunay triangulation can produce "slivers"—tetrahedra with near-coplanar vertices, small volumes, and vanishing angles—even for well-distributed point sets.

- $Re_x$: Local Reynolds number based on streamwise distance $x$, defined as $Re_x = U_{\infty} x / \nu$.
- $C_f$: Local skin-friction coefficient, defined as $C_f = 2\tau_w / (\rho U_{\infty}^2)$.
- $u_{\tau}$: Friction velocity, defined as $u_{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_w/\rho}$.

title: Isotropy of Euclidean Delaunay Meshing
content: The empty circumsphere criterion in Euclidean metric space minimizes the circumradius of elements relative to their edge lengths. This process statistically favors isotropic elements (approaching equilateral shapes) and strongly disfavors elements with high aspect ratios ($AR \gg 1$), unless the underlying metric space is explicitly modified (anisotropic meshing).

- $C_f$: Local skin-friction coefficient, defined as $C_f = 2\tau_w / (\rho U_{\infty}^2)$.

title: Hybrid Mesh Topology
content: To connect a structured layer of prismatic elements (quadrilateral bases, typically used for boundary layers) to an unstructured core of tetrahedral elements (triangular faces), transition elements are topologically required to match the faces. A pyramid element (quadrilateral base, triangular sides) provides a conformal interface between a prism stack and a tetrahedral volume.
notations:
- $Re_x$: Local Reynolds number based on streamwise distance $x$, defined as $Re_x = U_{\infty} x / \nu$.
- $C_f$: Local skin-friction coefficient, defined as $C_f = 2\tau_w / (\rho U_{\infty}^2)$.
- $u_{\tau}$: Friction velocity, defined as $u_{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_w/\rho}$.
